nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rivers, streams, ponds, and springs across a range of conditions — from clear upland tributaries to turbid lowland waterways; substrates of silt, sand, or stone; areas of both standing water and moderate current. Primarily in the Río Grande de Santiago basin and its tributaries in Nayarit and Jalisco.

decorPreferences: dense planting for cover and fry refuge

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods. A diet with a meaningful plant-based component is recommended — spirulina flakes, algae wafers, blanched spinach or kale, and vegetable-based pellets help prevent the gut issues associated with excessive animal-protein feeding. Supplement with high-quality flake, small pellets, and frozen foods such as daphnia or brine shrimp. Goodeids grazing on biofilm and algae in the tank is normal and beneficial.

goodWith: other goodeid species of similar size (species-only setups preferred)
robust short-finned livebearers in large tanks
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (fin nipping risk)
corydoras and small bottom-dwelling catfish (documented aggression including eye damage)

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen; standard FishBase image. Confirm availability and attribution before use.

summary: A hardy and adaptable Mexican goodeid livebearer bearing a striking orange-to-red caudal peduncle flush in males. Sensu stricto X. eiseni (post-2016 split) is restricted to the Río Grande de Santiago basin in Nayarit and Jalisco. IUCN Endangered. Viviparous with trophotaeniae; intermediate care due to unpredictable aggression.
careNotes:
Xenotoca eiseni (sensu stricto) is a goodeid livebearer from highland Mexico, distinguished from the related X. doadrioi (Etzatlán drainage, Jalisco) following the 2016 revision by Domínguez-Domínguez, Bernal-Zuñiga, and Piller (Zootaxa 4189). Hobbyist stock labelled "X. eiseni" predating 2016 may represent either species or a mix; provenance tracking is recommended for conservation-minded breeding programs.

This species is notable for being significantly hardier and more adaptable than many other goodeids. It tolerates a broad temperature range (15–30°C) and does not require a heater in typical room-temperature conditions in temperate climates. Cooler water (18–24°C) is often preferred for long-term health and breeding.

Behaviour can be highly variable: some individuals function well in community aquaria while others relentlessly nip fins or attack tankmates. The species has a well-documented aversion to corydoras and similar catfish, with recorded incidents of fin-stripping and eye damage. A species-only setup or a tank restricted to similarly robust, fast-moving fish is strongly advised. A ratio of approximately one male to two to three females helps disperse male harassment of individual females.

A plant-heavy diet is important; an excess of animal-based protein may cause bloating. Supplement flake and pellet foods with spirulina, algae wafers, and fresh or blanched greens.
breedingNotes: Livebearing with internal gestation via trophotaeniae — ribbon-like placenta-like structures that nourish developing embryos, analogous to mammalian placentation and unique to the Goodeidae. Gestation lasts approximately 7–8 weeks (roughly 60 days), after which 5–50 large, fully-formed fry are born measuring approximately 15 mm. Unlike poeciliid livebearers, female goodeids do not store sperm; each brood requires a separate mating. Fry are immediately self-sufficient and large enough to accept finely crushed flake from birth. Dense planting substantially improves fry survival as parents may consume small young. Breeding difficulty is rated easy when a stable, well-fed group is maintained in appropriate conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Xenotoca eiseni
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males 6 cm, females 7 cm), temperature 15–30°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 10–20 dH, minimum tank 55 L / 60×30×30 cm, temperament notes, breeding gestation 7–8 weeks, fry count 5–50, and trophotaeniae biology. Also confirmed species-tank recommendation and aggression toward corydoras.

additional.name: FishBase — Xenotoca eiseni
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males 6 cm, females 7 cm TL), temperature 15–32°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 9–19 dH, taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), IUCN Endangered status (EN, assessed 2018), and native range (Mexico, Central America). Confirmed females can produce young every ~60 days.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Red-tailed Goodeid
additional.notes: Supplementary trade care source. Confirmed temperature 15–30°C, pH 6.5–8.0, dH up to 20, adult size 7 cm, diet (flake, granules, pellets, algae wafers, frozen foods, leafy greens), temperament as “confident, nippy,” fry size ~15 mm, gestation ~60 days, and species-only recommendation.

additional.name: Goodeid Working Group — Xenotoca eiseni
additional.notes: Conservation-focused source. Provided population locality references (El Sacristán Tepic, Río Compostela in Nayarit), confirmed restricted sensu stricto range following 2016 split, and contextualised IUCN Endangered status. Image captions identified male/female specimens from Nayarit populations.

additional.name: Domínguez-Domínguez, Bernal-Zuñiga & Piller (2016) — Two new species of the genus Xenotoca (via GBIF/NovaTáxa summary)
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference for the 2016 three-way split of X. eiseni sensu lato into X. eiseni (Nayarit / Río Grande de Santiago), X. doadrioi (Etzatlán, Jalisco endorheic drainage), and X. lyonsi (Tamazula / Tuxpan rivers). Published in Zootaxa 4189(1): 81–98.

fish.breedingNotes: Internal gestation via trophotaeniae (~60 days); 5–50 large fry (~15 mm) per brood. Females do not store sperm — each brood requires mating. Dense planting recommended for fry survival. Fry accept finely crushed flake from birth.
